relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Jesus goes up to Jerusalem for a feast.
By the Sheep Gate, the pool of Bethesda holds waiting need.
Weakness, blindness, lameness, and withering gather there.
A later tradition explains the healing hope around stirred water.
One human has carried weakness for thirty-eight years.
Jesus sees, knows the long duration, and asks about wholeness.
The man has no one to carry him into the old healing system.
Jesus speaks direct rising, carrying, and walking.
Wholeness arrives immediately on Sabbath.
The healed one is challenged over carrying the mat.
He answers from the authority of the one who made him whole.
They seek the identity of the command.
The healed one does not yet know Jesus.
Jesus finds him in the temple and calls him to remain whole.
The man reports Jesus as the healer.
Sabbath healing becomes opposition.
Jesus names the Father’s ongoing work and his own working.
The conflict intensifies around Sabbath and Father-language.
The Son does what he sees the Father doing.
The Father loves the Son and shows greater works.
Life-giving belongs to Father and Son.
Judgment is entrusted to the Son.
Honoring the Son honors the Father who sent him.
Hearing and trusting crosses from death into life.
The hour now is when the dead hear and live.
Life in himself is given to the Son.
Judgment authority is given to the Son of Humanity.
The tombs will hear his voice.
Resurrection reveals the direction of practiced life.
His judgment is just because he seeks the Sender’s will.
Witness about self alone is not enough.
Another witness confirms him.
John witnessed to truth.
Human witness is not his need; saving/healing is theirs.
John was a burning and shining lamp.
The Father-given works witness more greatly.
The Father has borne witness, though they have not heard or seen.
The word does not remain where the sent one is not trusted.
Scripture bears witness beyond itself.
They search text yet refuse to come for life.
Jesus does not seek human glory.
He knows the absence of divine love within them.
The Father’s name is refused while self-name is received.
Seeking glory from one another blocks trust.
Moses, their hope, becomes the accuser.
Trusting Moses would open trust in Jesus.
Refusing Moses’ writings blocks trust in Jesus’ words.
